summary: Scorching China>Rider opener and rare St. Stephen with crisp SBD
blurb: The second set erupts with a blistering China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider transition and a rare Saint Stephen that lets Phil’s bass rumble. Jerry’s tone shines on the Not Fade Away jam, while the post‑drums segment builds intense momentum despite being cut short. Overall the performance is tight, energetic, and captured by an excellent Betty Cantor soundboard.
review: April 7, 1971 at Boston Music Hall finds the Dead in top form, delivering a first set that rocks from Me And My Uncle through Sugar Magnolia and then launching into a second‑set fireworks display. The show’s high point is the scorching China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider opener that rolls straight into a rare Saint Stephen, highlighted by Phil Lesh’s “rumbling the plant off the mantle” bass line and Jerry Garcia’s soaring tone. Not Fade Away erupts into an improvisational jam where Jerry’s guitar shines, and the post‑drums section builds a fierce groove before the tape cuts out, leaving listeners craving more. The set closes with a rollicking Johnny B. Goode, cementing this night as a solid, high‑energy snapshot of the early ’70s Dead, especially when heard through Betty Cantor’s pristine soundboard capture.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are explicitly positive, praising the pristine SBD sound and standout moments (rare St. Stephen, hot China>Rider). Two recordings are mixed but only note minor issues (jam after drums cut short) without detracting from overall performance quality. The consensus is a clearly upbeat sentiment, warranting a moderate positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Betty Cantor’s pristine soundboard capture delivers crystal‑clear audio, includes the rare Saint Stephen and scorching China>Rider sequence, and has no distortion or dropouts.

key_highlights: Rare Saint Stephen with Phil’s rumbling bass line.
Scorching China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider opener that sets the second set ablaze.
Post‑drums jam that builds intense momentum (though cut short).
Tight first‑set energy highlighted by Hard To Handle and Sugar Magnolia.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s tone is superb, especially on the Not Fade Away jam and throughout the rare Saint Stephen.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh delivers a rumbling bass line on Saint Stephen that anchors the band’s fire.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey drives the post‑drums energy with powerful percussion, propelling the jam forward.
band_performance.Billy: Bill locks in tight grooves, complementing Mickey and keeping the rhythmic foundation solid.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
